---
pagetitle: "jstats:Updating"
title-prefix: ""
---

## Updating

You're running three separate pieces of software: **R** (the engine that does the
statistics), **RStudio** (the window you drive it from), and **jstats** (the package
you added on top). They come from three different places, on three different
schedules, and — this is the part that trips people up — **updating one does not
update the others.**

The single most useful thing to know: **updating RStudio does not update R.** They
are separate programs from separate organizations, and the one that keeps *nagging*
you (RStudio pops up an "update available" box on startup) is the harmless one,
while the one that stays completely *silent* (R never tells you anything) is the one
with real consequences when it changes. So the noisy update is the safe one, and the
quiet update is the one to be a little careful with. Keep that backwards-feeling fact
in mind and the rest of this page follows from it.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Coming from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?"}
In commercial statistical software you update *the product* — one installer from one
vendor, everything at once. R works differently: the engine (R), the interface
(RStudio), and each add-on package (like jstats) are maintained by different people
and update on their own timelines. There's no single "update everything" button, and
that's normal. This page keeps the three tracks straight so you know which is which.
:::

## Which versions am I running?

Before updating anything, it helps to know where you stand. Run these in the RStudio
Console:

```r
R.version.string            # which version of R
RStudio.Version()$version   # which version of RStudio (only works inside RStudio)
packageVersion("jstats")    # which version of jstats
```

Each prints a version number — something like `R version 4.x.x`, an RStudio version,
and the jstats version. (If `RStudio.Version()` reports an error, you're not running
inside RStudio; the menu route **Help → About RStudio** works either way, and R
prints its own version in the Console every time it starts.) Jot the three down if
you're about to change anything — it makes "did that actually update?" easy to check
afterward.

## A rule of thumb: when to update

There's one rule worth remembering, and it splits by how much an update costs you:

- **Don't update R or RStudio in the middle of something.** Neither is urgent, and
  each costs you a little disruption — a restart, and for R a reinstall of your
  packages (below). Save them for *between* projects, or the start of a semester —
  not the week an assignment is due.
- **jstats is the exception.** Updating it is one command in a separate process, with
  nothing else disturbed, so it's safe to do almost any time — and if your instructor
  asks you to update jstats, do it whenever they ask — they sometimes know about an
  important new function that's just been added and want you using it. The one honest
  caveat while jstats is still pre-CRAN: an update can occasionally change how a
  function behaves, so if you're mid-way through something that's working, finish
  today's task first, *then* update.

## Updating jstats

This is the frequent, painless one. Because jstats is updated often during this
phase, it checks for a newer version each time you load it and prints a one-line
message at startup when one is available. The command is:

```r
jupdate()
```

That's the whole thing — one command, run in the Console, in its own separate
process. The full details (what it checks, why you restart R afterward, the
`ask = TRUE` option) live on the [Install jstats](install-jstats.qmd#keep-jstats-up-to-date)
page, since that's where you first meet it.

## Updating RStudio

RStudio checks for updates on startup and, when there's a newer version, pops up an
**Update Available** box. Two things make this less alarming than it looks:

**You don't have to take every update.** RStudio can put out a new version as often
as monthly, so they come around frequently, and skipping several costs you nothing. **Remind
Later** dismisses the box for now. If the reminders bother you, you can turn them off
for good: **Tools → Global Options → General**, and untick **Automatically notify me
of updates to RStudio**. (You'll still be able to update whenever you choose — you
just won't be prompted.)

**Updating RStudio never touches R or your packages.** It replaces the interface, not
the engine. Your R version, your installed packages, and jstats are all exactly where
they were. This is the safe update.

When you *do* want the new version, the one confusing step is the button labeled
**Quit and Download**. Despite the name, it doesn't download anything — it closes
RStudio and opens the Posit download page in your browser, where you scroll down to
the **Direct Downloads** section and pick the installer for your system yourself. The
awkward part is *finding* the right download; the install itself is a formality —
launch it, approve the administrator prompt (it names the publisher as *Posit
Software, PBC*), and click through the default screens to the end. When it's done,
open RStudio again and you're on the new version.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Aside: seeing what changed"}
Curious what a new RStudio version actually added? RStudio keeps its own list of
changes at **Help → What's New** — an in-app panel you can open any time. (R has no
equivalent, which is part of why R updates slip by unnoticed.)
:::

If a security box blocks the RStudio installer the first time you run it, that's the
same Windows warning covered in
[Troubleshooting](troubleshooting.qmd#security-warnings-when-downloading-or-installing).

## Updating R

Updating R is rare and deliberate — R never prompts you, so it only happens when you
decide to do it. Do it from CRAN the same way you installed it (see
[Install R and RStudio](install-r-and-rstudio.qmd)), between projects rather than
mid-task.

The one thing to know: **updating R starts you a fresh, empty package library**, so
jstats and everything else you'd installed have to be reinstalled afterward. That's
expected, not a fault — it's the "reinstall" cost mentioned in the rule of thumb
above. The one-time follow-up is the same install line you ran the first time; the
full walk-through, including how to tell this apart from a harmless warning, is under
[*there is no package called 'jstats'*](troubleshooting.qmd#starting-rstudio-and-loading-jstats)
on the Troubleshooting page.

There's also a reason updating R sometimes becomes necessary rather than optional.
jstats installs as a ready-made copy built for **recent** versions of R — roughly the
current release and the one before it. If your R is too old for a matching copy to
exist, the install can fail, or R may offer to build jstats slowly from source
instead (you'll see a prompt asking whether to *install from sources* — the answer is
still **No**). The fix in that case is exactly what you'd guess: **update R, then try
the install again.** Rather than track version numbers, just watch for the symptom —
if an install won't go through on an older R, updating R clears it.

## Setting up a new computer

Setting up jstats on a second machine is the same short routine, in order:

1. Install **R**, then **RStudio** — see [Install R and RStudio](install-r-and-rstudio.qmd).
2. Run the jstats install line in the Console:

```r
install.packages("jstats",
  repos = c("https://jma61.r-universe.dev", "https://cloud.r-project.org"))
library(jstats)
```

That's it — a fresh machine starts with an empty package library, so this is really
the *same* operation as recovering after an R update: a clean library, and one line
to put jstats back. Full detail is on the [Install jstats](install-jstats.qmd) page.
